signIn.desc: Sign in to keep your library, wishlist, and reading plans synced across all your devices.
signIn.guestPrompt: Prefer to try it offline first? You can also {link} (your data will live in this browser only).

about.title: About the {accent}
about.subtitle: Why this exists, what it does, and where it's going.
about.originBody1: Most readers I know have an Amazon wishlist, a Goodreads pile, a Notes app full of titles, a stack on the nightstand. Hundreds of books in queue — and somehow, every Friday night, we still spend twenty minutes deciding what to read next.
about.originBody2: The Oracle started as a personal experiment to fix that. I had a wishlist of around five hundred books spanning folk horror, gothic, Latin American literary fiction, Korean and Japanese literature, cozy fantasy, dark feminist gothic, body horror. A library bigger than I'd ever finish, and no way to use it as a decision tool.
about.originBody3: The premise: a wishlist shouldn't just be a hoarding pile. It should be the input to a recommendation engine that already knows your taste, because you built it yourself.
about.evolutionItem1Body: Instagram screenshots of book recommendations became styled HTML pages with Amazon search links. One file per session, no state, just a way to get titles into a wishlist faster.
about.evolutionItem2Body: A single-page app in plain JavaScript with localStorage. The first version of the Oracle's category and similarity matching. Worked, but didn't scale past one device.
about.evolutionItem3Body: The current version. React with Vite, Supabase for cross-device sync, Google authentication, the Anthropic API behind a Netlify function, Open Library for series detection. Plays well on phones, tablets, and laptops.
about.featuresIntro: Each feature is here to answer a different version of "what should I read next?"
about.featureWishlistBody: Your full pile of books you intend to read someday. Pre-seeded from a curated catalog, but fully editable — add a book manually with title, author, genre, description, and an optional Amazon link. Books leave the wishlist automatically when you mark them as read.
about.featureLibraryBody: Everything you've finished. Acts as memory: the Oracle uses your library to understand your taste when generating recommendations. You can import a Goodreads CSV in one shot from the Profile page.
about.featureReadNextBody: A short queue — the three or four books you've actually committed to next. Different from the wishlist, which is aspirational. When you finish one, it moves to the Library.
about.featureOracleBody: The recommendation engine, with two ways to draw books from the vault:
about.featureOracleCategoriesBody: Pick a temperament — folk horror, gothic, sapphic, Latin American — and the Oracle draws three books for you to choose from.
about.featureOracleSimilarBody: Tell the Oracle one to three books you've loved. It finds others with the same blood in them.
about.featureOracleModes: Both forks have two modes. Wishlist-only mode draws only from books you already own or own-in-spirit — fast, deterministic tag matching. AI-powered mode calls Claude and may suggest books beyond your wishlist, with reasoning.
about.featurePlansBody: A reading plan is a small, structured commitment: "finish this series," "read three books on grief," "work through this author's catalog." Plans are AI-generated from a prompt and persist across sessions. The "finish a series" plan type uses Open Library to fetch the books in order.
about.featureDashboardBody: The home screen. Shows your current Read Next queue, your active plan, and a quick path back to the Oracle. It's deliberately quiet — most of the action happens on the other pages.
about.roadmapBody: The Oracle isn't trying to become Goodreads. The destination is something smaller and more opinionated: a personal reading companion that knows your taste deeply, doesn't sell you anything, and helps you actually finish the books you said you'd read.
about.roadmapItem1: Reading-session notes and quotes, so the library becomes a place to think, not just a list.
about.roadmapItem2: Smarter "why you'd like this" reasoning that draws on your library, not just the prompt.
about.roadmapItem3: Shared plans — read a book with a friend, on the same schedule.
about.roadmapItem4: Better Spanish-language coverage for Costa Rican and Latin American editions.
about.feedbackBody: This is a prototype with real testers, and feedback shapes what gets built next. If something feels off, missing, or genuinely useful — say so.
about.roadmapTierNear1Body: Turn the series navigator into an active reading plan — ordered books, estimated time based on page count, progress tracking as you go.
about.roadmapTierNear2Body: Books read by genre, pages this year, series completion rates, reading pace. Your library as a mirror of your taste.
about.roadmapTierNear3Body: A private view for managing book reports, running targeted Oracle passes on flagged books, and keeping the catalog clean — without touching the database directly.
about.roadmapTierMed1Body: A short taste quiz that seeds your wishlist with Oracle-curated recommendations so your first session feels personal, not empty.
about.roadmapTierMed2Body: Individual book pages and reading plans at shareable URLs — so you can send someone exactly what you just finished.
about.roadmapTierMed3Body: Better duplicate detection, improved series handling, and progress feedback for large libraries. For the readers who want to bring everything over.
about.roadmapTierFuture1Body: Oracle-generated entry points into genres and movements — "Start here for Gothic horror" — built from the series and genre data already in the catalog.
about.roadmapTierFuture2Body: Oracle prompts in Spanish for Spanish-language books, significantly improving data quality for Latin American titles that Hardcover knows least well.
about.roadmapTierFuture3Body: Read a book with a friend on the same schedule. Shared progress, optional notes, no social feed.
